---
title: "gglm: A tool for teaching linear model diagnostics while adhering to the grammar of graphics"
author: Grayson White
image: featured.png
image-alt: "Screenshot from Colin's presentation with parsermd package logo overlaid."
---

## Abstract

In this talk, I'll discuss the gglm R package, which allows for easy creation of linear model diagnostic plots using the grammar of the graphics and ggplot2. 
More specifically, gglm provides a consistent and intuitive syntax to produce residual diagnostic plots for a variety of model types. 
We'll cover the basics of the package, along with motivation and use cases for a modern statistics, statistical learning, or data science classroom. 
We'll compare gglm's diagnostic plot solution to solutions provided by base R and others. 
We'll then discuss features of the package that allow for more robust analysis of residuals than typical model diagnostic tools, including different model types (i.e., engines) and multivariate diagnostics. 
Finally, I'll showcase a shiny dashboard that allows the user to upload data, build a statistical model, and produce diagnostics powered by gglm, aimed at introductory statistics courses that don't cover programming, but still want to use real data.  

## Slides

```{=html}
<iframe class="slide-deck" src="" height="420" width="747" style="border: 1px solid #2e3846;"></iframe>
```

[Source code for slides]()

[Full screen slides]()

## Speaker

![](https://graysonwhite.com/headshot.jpeg){style="float: right; padding-left: 10px;" fig-alt="Photo of Colin Rundel" width="200"}

Grayson White is a Visiting Assistant Professor of Statistics at Reed College.
His research interests are in small area estimation, hierarchical Bayesian modeling, environmental statistics applied to forestry and natural resources problems, and statistical software development. More specifically, my research focuses on implementing model-based statistical methods for small area estimation of forest attributes, and developing methodologies for formal assessment of those estimators.
